resume:
Priya Venkataraman
Product Designer | Fintech & Payments | Bengaluru / Remote

SUMMARY
Product designer with six years of experience shipping end-to-end flows for consumer fintech apps used by millions of monthly active users. I work fluently across discovery research, interaction design, prototyping, and design-system contribution, and I am happiest when I am sitting next to engineers reconciling the ideal flow with what the API can actually return this quarter. I care about measurable outcomes, not pretty mockups; every project below has a metric attached because I instrument my own flows in Amplitude and read the funnels myself rather than waiting for a PM to summarize them.

EXPERIENCE

Kite Money (Series C consumer payments app) — Product Designer — March 2021 to present
Kite is a peer-to-peer payments and bill-split app with roughly 4.1 million monthly active users at the time of writing. I am the sole designer on the Money Movement pod, which owns transfers, requests, and the shared-tab bill-splitting feature. I report to a design manager but sit inside a product trio with one PM and a tech lead.

- Led the end-to-end redesign of the peer-to-peer transfer confirmation flow. The legacy flow had a 22 percent drop-off on the final confirm screen, concentrated on first-time senders who hesitated at an ambiguous fee disclosure. I ran five moderated usability sessions, found that users could not tell whether the fee was already included in the amount, redesigned the screen to split principal and fee into two explicit lines with a plain-language tooltip, and shipped it behind an A/B test. Confirm-screen drop-off fell from 22 percent to 9 percent over the six-week test window, which the PM valued at roughly 180,000 dollars of incremental monthly transfer volume.
- Owned the shared-tab bill-splitting feature from a blank Figma file to general availability. I ran the discovery phase myself: twelve interviews with people who split rent and group-travel costs, a competitive teardown of four rival apps, and a card sort to settle the mental model of who-owes-whom. The feature launched in August 2022 and reached 310,000 monthly active users within two quarters, becoming the second-most-used surface in the app behind core transfers.
- Contributed twenty-three components to the Kite design system, including the money-input field that is now used on every screen that touches an amount. I wrote the usage documentation and the accessibility annotations for screen readers, and I pair-programmed the React Native implementation of the money-input with two engineers so the tokens matched exactly.

Loomtail Studio (digital product agency) — UX Designer — July 2019 to February 2021
Agency role serving early-stage startup clients, two to three engagements running in parallel at any time. This is where I learned to scope quickly and defend decisions to non-design stakeholders.

- Designed the onboarding flow for a meal-kit subscription client. Reduced steps from nine screens to five by deferring payment to after the plan-selection moment, and the client reported activation improving from 41 percent to 58 percent in the first month after launch. The deferral idea was contentious because the client's growth lead worried it would lower payment-capture rate; I framed it as a test rather than a permanent change, agreed on a guardrail metric, and only then got buy-in to ship it.
- Built the agency's first shared Figma component library so that four designers stopped rebuilding buttons on every project. Adoption was uneven at first; I ran two internal lunch-and-learns to get people to actually use it, and I wrote a one-page contribution guide so the library would not rot the moment I rolled off a project. By the time I left, three of the four designers were contributing back rather than only consuming.
- Designed a quiz-style product-recommendation flow for a skincare client. I learned the hard way here that a flow can test beautifully in moderated sessions and still underperform live, because my five testers were far more patient than real traffic; the unmoderated data showed people abandoning the quiz at question four. That lesson is why I now instrument and watch live funnels rather than trusting moderated sessions alone, and it directly shaped how I approached every project at Kite.

EARLY CAREER
Freelance and contract UI work, 2018 to 2019, mostly landing pages and marketing sites for local Bengaluru startups. This is where I built craft speed and learned to ship under tight deadlines with no design system to lean on, but it is not where I learned product thinking. I include it because it explains why I am fast in Figma and comfortable defending a decision to a founder who has strong opinions and no design background.

SKILLS
Figma (expert, including variables and auto-layout), prototyping in Figma and Principle, moderated and unmoderated usability research, Amplitude funnel analysis, basic SQL for pulling my own event data, React Native handoff fluency, WCAG accessibility annotation. I read research but I do not call myself a researcher; I call myself a designer who refuses to ship blind. I am comfortable partnering with a dedicated researcher when one exists, and equally comfortable running lightweight research myself when one does not, which on small teams is most of the time. I also care a great deal about copy: on a money screen, the words are the interface, and I would rather argue about a single label for an afternoon than ship something ambiguous.

HOW I WORK
I start from the funnel, not the mockup. Before I open Figma I want to know which step is leaking and for whom, because the data usually tells me where the real problem is and saves me from polishing a screen that was never the bottleneck. I prototype to test a hypothesis, not to produce a portfolio piece, and I am happy to ship something visually unremarkable if the funnel says it works. I keep engineers close from the first sketch, because I have wasted too many hours designing flows that the API could not support, and I would rather find that out on a whiteboard than in handoff. When I am wrong, I want to find out fast and cheaply, which is why I lean on small experiments and live instrumentation over big-bang launches.

EDUCATION
Bachelor of Design, Communication Design, National Institute of Design, 2018.

WHAT I AM LOOKING FOR
A mid-to-senior product design role where a single designer owns a real surface end to end, where engineering is in the room early, and where I am trusted to read the data myself. I am especially drawn to regulated or money-handling products where clarity is not a nicety but a requirement, because that is the kind of constraint that makes design decisions feel consequential. I am less interested in pixel-polish-only roles, in places where research is thrown over a wall, or in organizations where a designer is handed pre-decided requirements and asked only to make them pretty.
jd: Cardamom is hiring a Product Designer (mid-level, IC) for our Lending squad. Cardamom is a consumer credit app helping thin-file users build credit history; we have 1.2 million users and are growing the lending product from a single card into a small suite. You will be the second designer on a squad of six engineers and one PM, owning the application-and-approval flow end to end: discovery, interaction design, prototyping, and shipping with our React Native team. Concretely, in your first two quarters we want you to reduce application abandonment (currently 34 percent abandon before submitting) and improve the clarity of our APR and fee disclosures, which our support team says drive a meaningful share of tickets. We expect you to run your own usability research, instrument and read your own funnels, and contribute to our young design system. This is not a pure-visual role; we want someone who reasons about tradeoffs between the ideal flow and regulatory/engineering constraints. Comfort with regulated-product copy review (we have a compliance reviewer you will partner with) is a strong plus.

history.q: Tell me about your current role at Kite — what surface do you own and who do you work with day to day?
Your resume leads with the transfer confirmation redesign. Walk me through the problem as you first understood it.
How did you figure out that the fee disclosure specifically was the thing causing drop-off, versus some other friction?
Walk me through the actual design change you shipped, screen by screen.
How was the change rolled out and measured — what was the experiment setup?
You said drop-off went from 22 percent to 9 percent. How do you know the redesign caused that and not seasonality or another concurrent change?
Were there any tradeoffs you had to make on that screen — anything you wanted to do but couldn't?
Our role is regulated lending with APR disclosures. How would you approach a fee-clarity problem when a compliance reviewer can veto your copy?
Give me the full arc of one project where you owned discovery through launch — start at the blank file.

candidate_last_answer: Sure — the shared-tab bill-splitting feature is the cleanest example because I owned it from an empty Figma file. Situation: Kite had strong solo transfers but no way to split a group bill, and group spend was the top unmet request in our app-store reviews. Task: as the sole designer on Money Movement, I had to take it from zero to GA, and the PM and I agreed the bar was reaching meaningful adoption within two quarters without cannibalizing core transfers. Action: I ran the discovery myself — twelve interviews with people who split rent and travel, a teardown of four competitors, and a card sort to lock the who-owes-whom mental model, which is where I learned people think in terms of a running tab, not individual IOUs, so I designed around a persistent shared tab. I prototyped three flows, tested them in five moderated sessions, then pair-designed the React Native build with two engineers and wrote the accessibility annotations. Result: it launched in August 2022 and reached 310,000 monthly active users within two quarters, becoming the second-most-used surface behind core transfers, and core-transfer volume held flat so we confirmed no cannibalization. If I had to name the lever, the card sort was the turning point — without it I'd have shipped an IOU model that tested badly.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: the card sort was the turning point

expected-yield: the specific card-sort findings and the decision rule she used to reject the IOU model and adopt the running-tab model, including what the sort actually showed and how she weighed it against the prototype test results
